schools.summary: Gill, Calvin, and Henry: though a real serpent (the most subtil of the beasts) was the instrument, the true tempter was Satan, the devil, who used and indwelt the serpent (Gill: 'a real serpent... but through Satan being in it, and using it'; Henry: 'the tempter was the devil, in the shape and likeness of a serpent'; 'that old serpent, called the Devil, and Satan,' Revelation 12:9; 20:2); the serpent opening with a crafty, insinuating question, 'Yea, hath God said, Ye shall not eat of every tree of the garden?', casting doubt on God's word and goodness (subtly misrepresenting God's command, which forbade only one tree, 2:16-17, suggesting God grudges man the trees, the first temptation beginning by undermining trust in God's word and benevolence); Satan's policy to enter into talk with the woman (the weaker, and alone), to question God's word, and to insinuate doubt — the serpent's subtlety the craft of the tempter beginning the assault on man's innocence.

Keil and Delitzsch, with Calvin: the serpent's question is crafty and insinuating, casting doubt on God's word and goodness, misrepresenting the command (which forbade only one tree) as though God grudged man the trees; the temptation begins by undermining trust in God. A second view takes Satan as appearing in the form or likeness of a serpent; a rationalist reading takes the serpent as merely a literal beast — which the expositors reject, since a beast could not so speak and reason, the speech and craft betraying a higher, evil intelligence, the dominant reading being Satan the true tempter working through a real serpent.

debates.question: Who is the tempter — the serpent, or Satan?

sides.position: Satan, the devil, the true tempter, using and indwelling a real serpent as his instrument (the dominant reading)
Satan appearing in the form or likeness of a serpent
A literal serpent alone (rationalist) — rejected, since a beast could not so speak and reason
holders: the rationalist reading (rejected by the expositors)

themes: the serpent was more subtil than any beast of the field... Yea, hath God said, Ye shall not eat of every tree of the garden?
the subtil serpent and the first temptation
the crux of the tempter's identity (Satan using a real serpent)
the temptation beginning by casting doubt on God's word and goodness

schools.summary: The woman's answer: 'and the woman said unto the serpent, We may eat of the fruit of the trees of the garden' — the woman answering the serpent's question (v.1), affirming that they may eat of the trees of the garden (beginning to correct the serpent's misrepresentation, acknowledging God's permission to eat of the trees); the woman rightly affirming the permission to eat (2:16), yet, by entering into parley with the serpent, already stepping onto dangerous ground (the conversation with the tempter the beginning of her peril); the woman's engaging the serpent in dialogue, rather than fleeing or refusing, exposing her to the temptation (she beginning to weigh the tempter's words, though she states the permission correctly the conversation opening the way for the serpent's further assault, vv.3-5, the danger of parleying with temptation) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: the woman said unto the serpent, We may eat of the fruit of the trees of the garden
the woman's answer to the serpent
the woman affirming God's permission yet parleying with the tempter
the danger of the dialogue with temptation

schools.summary: The woman's statement of the prohibition: 'but of the fruit of the tree which is in the midst of the garden, God hath said, Ye shall not eat of it, neither shall ye touch it, lest ye die' — the woman stating the prohibition concerning the forbidden tree, recounting God's command though with notable alterations from the original (2:17); 'neither shall ye touch it' the addition not in God's original command (2:17, which forbade only eating, whether her own gloss adding to God's word, or a sign of her wavering, marking a departure from the exact word of God, some seeing in it the beginning of her error — adding to God's command as the serpent led her to question it); 'lest ye die' a weaker form than God's emphatic 'thou shalt surely die' (2:17, she softening the certainty of the threat, the alterations — adding 'touch,' weakening 'surely die' — showing her grasp of God's word already loosening under the serpent's influence, the diminished sense of the command preparing for her fall) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: of the fruit of the tree which is in the midst of the garden, God hath said, Ye shall not eat of it, neither shall ye touch it, lest ye die
the woman's statement of the prohibition with an addition
the addition 'touch it' and the weakened threat
the woman's grasp of God's word loosening under the serpent

schools.summary: The serpent's denial: 'and the serpent said unto the woman, Ye shall not surely die' — the serpent now boldly contradicting God's word (where God said 'thou shalt surely die,' 2:17, the serpent flatly denying it, the temptation moving from insinuating doubt, v.1, to open denial of God's threat, the first lie, the direct contradiction of God); 'Ye shall not surely die' a direct, brazen lie (the contradiction of God's express word, the serpent, Satan, 'a liar, and the father of it,' John 8:44, denying the penalty of sin, persuading the woman that God's threat is empty, the lie undermining the fear of God's judgment); the serpent's flat denial assaulting God's truthfulness (charging God, in effect, with a false threat, the temptation now openly opposing God's word with a lie, emboldening the woman to disbelieve the consequence of sin, the denial of death the heart of the deception) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: the serpent said unto the woman, Ye shall not surely die
the serpent's bold denial of God's word
the direct lie denying the penalty of sin
the assault on God's truthfulness

schools.summary: The serpent's promise: 'for God doth know that in the day ye eat thereof, then your eyes shall be opened, and ye shall be as gods, knowing good and evil' — the serpent insinuating that God's prohibition springs from envy (God knows the fruit will open their eyes and make them like himself, and forbids it to keep them down, the charge that God grudges man advancement, the slander of God's goodness and motive); 'ye shall be as gods (or as God, Elohim), knowing good and evil' the lure (the bait the promise of godlike knowledge and elevation, the temptation appealing to pride and the desire to be like God, independent of him, the lure of forbidden wisdom and self-exaltation, the very sin by which Satan fell); the serpent's promise a deceptive half-truth (their eyes indeed opened, 3:7, and they came to know good and evil, 3:22, but not as gods, rather as guilty sinners who lost their innocence, the promise fulfilled in a ruinous not a glorious way, the deception in the false hope of gain) — Gill, Ellicott, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: God doth know that in the day ye eat thereof, then your eyes shall be opened, and ye shall be as gods, knowing good and evil
the serpent's promise of godlike knowledge
the lure of godlike wisdom and self-exaltation
the deceptive half-truth charging God with envy

schools.summary: The fall: 'and when the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was pleasant to the eyes, and a tree to be desired to make one wise, she took of the fruit thereof, and did eat, and gave also unto her husband with her; and he did eat' — the woman, persuaded by the serpent, seeing the tree as 'good for food (the lust of the flesh), pleasant to the eyes (the lust of the eyes), and... to be desired to make one wise (the pride of life)' — the threefold temptation, cf. 1 John 2:16, appealing to appetite, beauty, and the desire for wisdom, the fruit now appearing desirable on every count, the woman's desire kindled; 'she took of the fruit thereof, and did eat' (the woman yielding, the act of disobedience, the entrance of sin, the root of the fall unbelief — disbelieving God's word and threat — and pride — desiring to be as God); 'gave also unto her husband with her; and he did eat' (the woman giving the fruit to Adam, who ate, Adam not deceived as the woman, 1 Timothy 2:14, yet eating, joining in the transgression, by Adam's sin as the head of the race sin and death entering the world, Romans 5:12, the fall of mankind) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: the tree was good for food, and... pleasant to the eyes, and a tree to be desired to make one wise, she took of the fruit thereof, and did eat, and gave also unto her husband
the fall - the woman and the man eating the forbidden fruit
the threefold temptation yielded to, the disobedience committed
sin and death entering the world by Adam's transgression

schools.summary: The opened eyes and shame: 'and the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked; and they sewed fig leaves together, and made themselves aprons' — their eyes indeed opened (as the serpent promised, v.5) but not to godlike glory (they coming to a guilty, shameful self-knowledge, perceiving their nakedness, now a source of shame, contrast 2:25, the loss of innocence bringing the consciousness of guilt and shame); 'they knew that they were naked' the knowledge of their lost innocence (sin bringing shame, their consciences accusing them, the nakedness, before without shame, 2:25, now exposing their guilt, the entrance of shame the immediate fruit of sin); 'they sewed fig leaves together, and made themselves aprons' (seeking to cover their shame with fig leaves, the figure of man's vain attempt to cover his guilt by his own devices, the fig-leaf aprons an inadequate covering, the futile effort of fallen man to hide his sin, only God's covering, the coats of skins, v.21, sufficing) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill.

themes: the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked; and they sewed fig leaves together, and made themselves aprons
the opened eyes, the sense of shame, and the fig-leaf covering
the guilty self-knowledge of lost innocence
the vain attempt of fallen man to cover his guilt

schools.summary: The guilty pair hiding: 'and they heard the voice of the LORD God walking in the garden in the cool of the day: and Adam and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the LORD God amongst the trees of the garden' — they hearing the voice (or sound) of the LORD God walking in the garden in the cool of the evening (God's approach, the sound of his presence, God coming to the garden to call the fallen pair to account, the once-welcome presence of God now dreaded); 'Adam and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the LORD God' (guilt driving them to hide from God, the folly and fear of sin that thinks to hide from the omniscient, omnipresent God, the once-loved fellowship with God now flight and concealment, the conscience of the guilty fleeing from God); the hiding marking the alienation sin has caused (fear, shame, and estrangement having replaced the innocent fellowship with God, the fallen pair who once delighted in God's presence now fleeing it, sin separating man from God, the vain attempt to hide showing the folly and misery of the guilty conscience) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill.

themes: they heard the voice of the LORD God walking in the garden in the cool of the day: and Adam and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the LORD God
the guilty pair hiding from God's presence
guilt driving them to flee the once-loved fellowship with God
sin separating man from God

schools.summary: God's call to Adam: 'and the LORD God called unto Adam, and said unto him, Where art thou?' — God calling to the hiding Adam, 'Where art thou?' not a question of ignorance (God knew where Adam was) but a searching, summoning call (to bring Adam out of his hiding, to convict him, and to call him to account, the question awakening conscience and inviting confession); God's calling Adam, rather than destroying him at once, an act of grace (God seeking the fallen sinner, the call the first step of God's gracious dealing with fallen man, God coming seeking as he would seek the lost throughout Scripture, mercy mingled with the judgment); 'Where art thou?' the convicting summons (summoning Adam to consider his state, where he has brought himself by sin, fallen from innocence and fellowship into guilt and hiding, the question meant to bring Adam to self-examination, confession, and the sense of his lost condition, God's searching word calling the sinner to account) — Gill, Calvin, Henry, Wesley.

themes: the LORD God called unto Adam, and said unto him, Where art thou?
God's call to the hiding Adam
the searching, summoning call to convict and call to account
God's gracious seeking of the fallen sinner

schools.summary: Adam's confession of fear: 'and he said, I heard thy voice in the garden, and I was afraid, because I was naked; and I hid myself' — Adam answering God's call, confessing that he heard God's voice, was afraid, and hid because of his nakedness (the answer betraying his guilt, the fear, shame, and hiding the marks of a guilty conscience, the once-confident fellowship now fear and concealment); 'I was afraid, because I was naked' (Adam's fear and sense of nakedness the fruit of sin, the guilty conscience dreading God's presence, the nakedness he had not noticed in innocence, 2:25, now filling him with fear and shame, the fear betraying the change sin has wrought, the sinner dreading the God he once loved); 'I hid myself' (Adam admitting his hiding, yet his answer, while confessing fear and hiding, not yet confessing the sin itself, the eating, he speaking of the symptom — shame, fear — but not the cause — disobedience — the partial confession of the guilty who feels the effect of sin but is slow to own the sin) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: I heard thy voice in the garden, and I was afraid, because I was naked; and I hid myself
Adam's confession of fear and hiding
the fear, shame, and hiding betraying a guilty conscience
the partial confession that owns the effect but not the sin

schools.summary: God's probing question: 'and he said, Who told thee that thou wast naked? Hast thou eaten of the tree, whereof I commanded thee that thou shouldest not eat?' — God pressing Adam further, 'Who told thee that thou wast naked?' (the sense of nakedness and shame not natural but the fruit of guilt, the question drawing Adam toward the real issue, how he came to this shameful self-knowledge, God leading him to confront the cause); 'Hast thou eaten of the tree, whereof I commanded thee that thou shouldest not eat?' the direct charge (God bringing the matter to a point, the transgression of his express command, the question meant to elicit confession, God naming the sin — eating of the forbidden tree against his command — pressing Adam to own his disobedience); God's questions (vv.9, 11) designed to bring Adam to confession and repentance (God dealing with the sinner not by immediate destruction but by inquiry, that he may acknowledge his sin, the gracious method of conviction leading the guilty to own his transgression) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Who told thee that thou wast naked? Hast thou eaten of the tree, whereof I commanded thee that thou shouldest not eat?
God's probing question about the tree
God naming the sin to elicit confession
the gracious method of conviction leading the guilty to own his sin

schools.summary: Adam shifts the blame: 'and the man said, The woman whom thou gavest to be with me, she gave me of the tree, and I did eat' — Adam, instead of frankly confessing his sin, shifting the blame to the woman ('she gave me of the tree') and, by implication, to God himself ('the woman whom thou gavest to be with me'), excusing himself, accusing both his wife and his Maker (the evasion and self-justification of the guilty); 'the woman whom thou gavest' subtly casting blame on God (as if God, by giving him the woman, were the occasion of his sin, the ingratitude and impudence of fallen man, who blames God's good gift for his own fault, the root of self-justification that will not own its guilt); 'and I did eat' the reluctant admission (Adam finally admitting the act, yet only after shifting the blame, his confession grudging and self-excusing, owning the act but not the guilt, the manner of fallen man who confesses under pressure but seeks to transfer the fault, the failure of true repentance) — Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: The woman whom thou gavest to be with me, she gave me of the tree, and I did eat
Adam blaming the woman and God
the self-justification of fallen man, casting blame on God's gift
the grudging admission that owns the act but not the guilt

schools.summary: God questions the woman: 'and the LORD God said unto the woman, What is this that thou hast done? And the woman said, The serpent beguiled me, and I did eat' — God turning to the woman with a searching question, and she, like Adam, shifting the blame to the serpent ('the serpent beguiled me'), each accusing another, none frankly owning the guilt, the pattern of excuse continuing; 'What is this that thou hast done?' the convicting question (pressing the woman to consider the gravity of her deed, the question a rebuke and a call to confession, God bringing her too to account); 'The serpent beguiled (deceived) me, and I did eat' (the woman blaming the serpent for deceiving her, though it is true she was deceived, 1 Timothy 2:14, she, like Adam, owning the act, 'I did eat,' only while transferring the fault, yet her statement, 'the serpent beguiled me,' true and preparing for the sentence on the serpent, v.14, the guilty seeking to excuse but God judging each in turn) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: What is this that thou hast done? And the woman said, The serpent beguiled me, and I did eat
God questioning the woman and her blaming the serpent
the continued pattern of excuse, owning the act while transferring the fault
her true word preparing for God's sentence on the serpent

schools.summary: The curse on the serpent: 'and the LORD God said unto the serpent, Because thou hast done this, thou art cursed above all cattle, and above every beast of the field; upon thy belly shalt thou go, and dust shalt thou eat all the days of thy life' — God, without questioning the serpent (the tempter, not invited to confess but condemned), pronouncing the curse: the serpent cursed above all the creatures (the instrument of the temptation judged, the curse marking the degradation and abhorrence of the serpent); 'upon thy belly shalt thou go, and dust shalt thou eat all the days of thy life' (the serpent condemned to crawl on its belly and eat dust, whether marking a change in the serpent's form, or more likely using the serpent's natural mode as the perpetual sign of its degradation and humiliation, the creature made a token of abasement and loathing); beneath the curse on the literal serpent lying the sentence on Satan, the true tempter (the serpent's crawling and dust-eating figuring Satan's degradation, defeat, and abasement, the curse on the serpent the prelude to the promise of his ultimate overthrow, v.15, the literal and the spiritual sentence joined) — Gill, Clarke, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Because thou hast done this, thou art cursed above all cattle... upon thy belly shalt thou go, and dust shalt thou eat
the curse on the serpent
the instrument of the temptation degraded and humiliated
beneath the figure, Satan the tempter sentenced to abasement

schools.summary: Gill and Calvin (the Messianic protevangelium): 'I will put enmity between thee and the woman, and between thy seed and her seed; it shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel' — God setting a perpetual enmity between the serpent (Satan) and the woman, and between their seeds (the seed of the serpent, the wicked/children of the devil, and the seed of the woman, the godly and supremely Christ); the first gospel promise (the protevangelium): the 'seed of the woman' supremely Christ, born of the woman (the virgin), who would bruise (crush) the serpent's head, destroying Satan's power and lordship by his death and resurrection (Romans 16:20; Hebrews 2:14; 1 John 3:8); 'thou shalt bruise his heel' the suffering Christ would endure (the wounding of the cross, a heel-wound not a mortal head-wound); the singular 'it/He' (the seed) pointing to the one Redeemer, the conquering seed Christ, in whom also the spiritual seed (the church) overcome the serpent.

Clarke and Wesley: the verse is the first promise of the gospel, the seed of the woman being Jesus Christ, born of a virgin, who bruises the serpent's head — destroys his power and lordship — by his death; the heel-bruising the sufferings of Christ and his people; the promise the dawn of redemption immediately after the fall, the hope held out to fallen man of the Deliverer who would overcome the tempter. A collective reading takes the 'seed of the woman' as mankind generally in perpetual conflict with the serpent's seed, but the Christian tradition reads it Messianically, fulfilled in Christ; the textual note: the Hebrew 'hu' ('it/He') and the Vulgate's 'ipsa' ('she,' a Marian reading) vs the better 'ipse/He' (Christ).

debates.question: Who is the 'seed of the woman' that bruises the serpent's head?

sides.position: Supremely Christ — the Messianic protevangelium, the first gospel promise, fulfilled in Christ's victory over Satan by his death (the Christian reading)
Mankind collectively — the human race in perpetual conflict with the serpent's seed (the collective reading)

themes: I will put enmity between thee and the woman, and between thy seed and her seed; it shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel
the protevangelium - the seed of the woman bruising the serpent's head
the crux of the first gospel promise (Christ vs mankind collectively)
the promise of the Redeemer who would crush Satan

schools.summary: The sentence on the woman: 'unto the woman he said, I will greatly multiply thy sorrow and thy conception; in sorrow thou shalt bring forth children; and thy desire shall be to thy husband, and he shall rule over thee' — the woman's sentence the multiplied sorrow and pain of conception and childbirth (the pains of childbearing the consequence of sin upon the woman, what should have been pure joy now mingled with sorrow and labor); the multiplied sorrow the woman's chastening for her part in the fall (yet mingled with mercy, the blessing of children, and the hope of the promised seed, v.15, through childbearing, the sentence both judgment and, through it, the way of the promise); 'thy desire shall be to thy husband, and he shall rule over thee' (the woman placed under the rule and headship of her husband, whether the loving subordination established at creation now made harder by sin, or a new subjection consequent on the fall, the woman's desire toward and dependence on her husband, and his rule over her, marking the disordered relation that sin introduced, the headship of the husband part of the woman's sentence) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: I will greatly multiply thy sorrow and thy conception; in sorrow thou shalt bring forth children; and thy desire shall be to thy husband, and he shall rule over thee
the sentence on the woman - sorrow in childbearing and subjection
the pain of childbearing the consequence of sin, mingled with mercy
the woman placed under her husband's rule

schools.summary: The sentence on Adam: 'and unto Adam he said, Because thou hast hearkened unto the voice of thy wife, and hast eaten of the tree, of which I commanded thee, saying, Thou shalt not eat of it: cursed is the ground for thy sake; in sorrow shalt thou eat of it all the days of thy life' — Adam's sin named: he listened to his wife rather than to God and ate the forbidden fruit (his disobedience to God's express command the ground of his sentence, he preferring his wife's voice to God's); 'cursed is the ground for thy sake' (the earth, on man's account, cursed, the ground which man tills brought under a curse because of his sin, the creation subjected to vanity through man's fall, Romans 8:20, man's environment sharing in the consequences of his sin); 'in sorrow shalt thou eat of it all the days of thy life' (man's food, before freely given, 1:29; 2:16, now to be won by sorrow and toil, labor, before a happy employment, 2:15, becoming painful, wearisome toil, the curse turning man's work into hardship, man eating his bread in sorrow all his days) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Because thou hast hearkened unto the voice of thy wife, and hast eaten of the tree... cursed is the ground for thy sake; in sorrow shalt thou eat of it all the days of thy life
the sentence on Adam and the curse on the ground
the earth cursed for man's sin, the creation subjected to vanity
man's food won by sorrow and toil

schools.summary: The thorns of the cursed ground: 'thorns also and thistles shall it bring forth to thee; and thou shalt eat the herb of the field' — the cursed ground (v.17) now bringing forth thorns and thistles (the earth, which should yield fruit, producing weeds and useless growth, the resistance and barrenness of the ground part of the curse, man's labor made harder by the thorns the earth now bears); the thorns and thistles marking the earth's changed condition under the curse (its grudging, resistant fruitfulness, man struggling against the weeds, the ground no longer yielding its produce freely but with difficulty and against opposition, the figure of the toil and frustration of fallen labor); 'thou shalt eat the herb of the field' (man, who in Eden ate of the garden's trees, now eating the herb of the field, the produce of his toilsome cultivation, his food the grain and herbs won by labor from the cursed ground, the change from the garden's bounty to the field's hard-won produce, man eating by the sweat of his brow, v.19) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: Thorns also and thistles shall it bring forth to thee; and thou shalt eat the herb of the field
the thorns and thistles of the cursed ground
the earth's resistant, grudging fruitfulness under the curse
man's food won by toil against the weeds

schools.summary: The toil and return to dust: 'in the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat bread, till thou return unto the ground; for out of it wast thou taken: for dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return' — man now winning his bread by hard, sweating labor (his food the fruit of toil, the curse turning work into wearisome, exhausting labor, man eating by the sweat of his brow all his days); 'till thou return unto the ground; for out of it wast thou taken' (man's toil continuing until death, when he returns to the ground from which he was formed, 2:7, death, the wages of sin, now man's lot, the body returning to the earth whence it came, the sentence of mortality); 'for dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return' (man, formed of dust, 2:7, shall return to dust, death and the dissolution of the body pronounced, the threatened death, 2:17, now taking hold, man mortal, his body to decay and return to the earth, yet the promise of the seed, v.15, holding out hope beyond death) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: In the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat bread, till thou return unto the ground... for dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return
the toil of man and the return to dust
the wearisome toil of fallen labor and the mortality of man
the threatened death taking hold, under the hope of the promised seed

schools.summary: Adam names his wife Eve: 'and Adam called his wife's name Eve; because she was the mother of all living' — Adam naming his wife 'Eve' (Chavvah, 'life' or 'living'), because she would be the mother of all living human beings (the name marking her as the ancestress of the whole race, despite the sentence of death, v.19, Adam naming his wife 'life,' looking to the continuance of mankind through her); Adam's naming his wife 'Eve' (life), just after the sentence of death, an act of faith and hope (laying hold of the promise of the seed, v.15, through Eve, the mother of all living, would come the living seed, the Redeemer, the name expressing hope in the promise amid the sentence of death, faith looking beyond death to the life promised); the naming marking the continuance of the race and the channel of the promise (though death has entered, life will continue through Eve, and through her line the promised seed, Adam's faith grasping the hope of life amid the curse) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: Adam called his wife's name Eve; because she was the mother of all living
Adam naming his wife Eve, mother of all living
the naming an act of faith amid the sentence of death
laying hold of the promise of the seed, the living Redeemer through her

schools.summary: God clothes the pair: 'unto Adam also and to his wife did the LORD God make coats of skins, and clothed them' — God himself making coats of skins for Adam and Eve and clothing them (in place of their inadequate fig-leaf aprons, v.7, God providing a fitting covering, the divine provision for man's shame far better than man's own devising, God clothing the fallen pair); the coats of skins, requiring the death of animals, widely seen as the first instance of death and, by implication, of sacrifice (the skins from slain beasts pointing to the covering of sin by the death of a substitute, the figure, in the typological reading, of the covering of sin by sacrifice, fulfilled in Christ, the righteousness that clothes the sinner, God's clothing an act of mercy and a token of the way of atonement); God's clothing the pair an act of grace and care toward the fallen (he not abandoning them in their shame but providing for them, the covering of their nakedness a token of his continuing mercy, even under the sentence God showing kindness, the clothing prefiguring the better covering of grace) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: Unto Adam also and to his wife did the LORD God make coats of skins, and clothed them
God clothing the pair with coats of skins
the coats of skins, the first death/sacrifice, figuring the covering of sin
God's act of grace and mercy toward the fallen

schools.summary: Man become as one of us: 'and the LORD God said, Behold, the man is become as one of us, to know good and evil: and now, lest he put forth his hand, and take also of the tree of life, and eat, and live for ever' — God speaking, perhaps with a holy irony, of man's having become 'as one of us' in knowing good and evil (the serpent's promise, 3:5, 'ye shall be as gods, knowing good and evil,' in a sad sense fulfilled, man now knowing good and evil but as a guilty sinner, having lost the good and embraced the evil, the 'us,' as in 1:26, marking the divine plurality, the Trinity, or the divine counsel); the words carrying a tone of solemn irony or reproach (man, who sought to be as God, having gained a knowledge of good and evil but to his ruin, the bitter fulfillment of the serpent's lie, man's exaltation his fall); 'lest he put forth his hand, and take also of the tree of life, and eat, and live for ever' (God acting to prevent man, now fallen, from eating of the tree of life and so living forever in his sinful, miserable state, the bar from the tree of life, in mercy as well as judgment, that man should not be immortalized in sin, preparing for the way of redemption and a better, eternal life through the promised seed) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Behold, the man is become as one of us, to know good and evil: and now, lest he put forth his hand, and take also of the tree of life, and eat, and live for ever
God's word that man has become as one of us, and the bar from the tree of life
the sad, ironic fulfillment of the serpent's promise
the merciful bar from the tree of life, lest man be immortalized in sin

schools.summary: Man sent from Eden: 'therefore the LORD God sent him forth from the garden of Eden, to till the ground from whence he was taken' — God sending man out of the garden (the fallen man no longer to dwell in the paradise of God, sent to till the ordinary ground, the cursed earth, v.17-19, from which he was formed, 2:7, the loss of Eden the consequence of sin); man's being 'sent forth' from Eden the loss of the place of innocence, fellowship, and blessing (the fallen man excluded from paradise, the expulsion both judgment, the loss of the garden, and the appointed lot of fallen man, to live and labor outside Eden); 'to till the ground from whence he was taken' (man set to till the cursed ground, from which his body was formed, his appointed work now the toilsome cultivation of the earth, v.19, man returning as it were to the dust from which he came, to labor upon it until he returns to it in death, the life of fallen man labor on the cursed ground) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill.

themes: the LORD God sent him forth from the garden of Eden, to till the ground from whence he was taken
man sent forth from Eden to till the ground
the loss of paradise the consequence of sin
the appointed lot of fallen man, to labor on the cursed earth

schools.summary: The driving out and the cherubim: 'so he drove out the man; and he placed at the east of the garden of Eden Cherubims, and a flaming sword which turned every way, to keep the way of the tree of life' — beyond merely sending him forth (v.23), God driving man out, expelling him decisively from the garden (the stronger word marking the finality and force of the expulsion, fallen man removed from paradise and barred from return); God setting 'at the east of the garden of Eden Cherubims, and a flaming sword which turned every way' (the cherubim, the angelic guardians, and a flaming, whirling sword at the east of the garden, the cherubim and sword barring the way to the garden and the tree of life, the figure of the angelic guard and the unapproachable holiness barring fallen man from paradise); 'to keep (guard) the way of the tree of life' (the cherubim and sword guarding the way to the tree of life, that fallen man may not return and eat, v.22, the access to the tree of life barred, the way to life closed to fallen man by his own works but, in the larger redemptive story, to be reopened through the promised seed, the tree of life reappearing in the New Jerusalem, Revelation 22:2, restored to the redeemed) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: he drove out the man; and he placed at the east of the garden of Eden Cherubims, and a flaming sword which turned every way, to keep the way of the tree of life
the driving out of man and the cherubim guarding the way to the tree of life
fallen man barred from paradise and the tree of life
the way to life closed by sin, to be reopened through the promised seed
